title: Lesson 46 — The Reformer

words.definition: a person who attacks or criticizes cherished beliefs or institutions
words.example: The architect was an iconoclast who rejected every convention.
Her radical proposal would rewrite the whole system.

words.definition: opposing political or social progress or reform
words.example: Reactionary critics wanted to undo every recent change.

words.definition: authorized or generally accepted theory, doctrine, or practice
words.example: He challenged the economic orthodoxy of his time.

words.definition: a belief that strongly conflicts with accepted opinion
words.example: Questioning the founder's plan was treated as heresy.

words.definition: a principle laid down by an authority as incontrovertibly true
words.example: He refused to accept the dogma without evidence.

words.definition: a belief or set of beliefs held and taught by a group
words.example: The party's economic doctrine went unchanged for decades.

words.definition: a system of ideas and ideals forming the basis of a theory or policy
words.example: The two parties disagreed on nearly every point of ideology.

words.definition: seeking to undermine an established system or institution
words.example: Officials banned the pamphlet as subversive literature.

words.definition: a person who opposes official policy, especially of an authoritarian state
words.example: The exiled dissident continued to write from abroad.

words.definition: a person fighting against an established government or authority
words.example: Insurgents controlled the mountain passes for months.

words.definition: a person who renounces a former belief or allegiance
words.example: His old party branded him an apostate for switching sides.

story: The young professor was branded an [[iconoclast|iconoclast]] the moment she published her thesis. Her ideas were [[radical|radical]] enough to alarm the department's most [[reactionary|reactionary]] elders, who accused her of attacking their whole [[orthodoxy|orthodoxy]]. To question the founding theory, they said, was practically [[heresy|heresy]] — she had broken the field's oldest [[dogma|dogma]] and defied its official [[doctrine|doctrine]]. Younger colleagues, drawn to her [[ideology|ideology]], called her writing bracing rather than [[subversive|subversive]]; older ones called her a [[dissident|dissident]], even an [[insurgent|insurgent]] against the discipline, and a few muttered the word [[apostate|apostate]] behind her back.
